h1Title: Hypopituitarism: Symptoms, Causes, Diagnosis, and Treatment
heroOverview: Hypopituitarism is a disorder of the pituitary gland characterized by deficiency of one or more anterior pituitary hormones — including ACTH (adrenocorticotropic hormone), TSH (thyroid-stimulating hormone), FSH/LH (gonadotropins), GH (growth hormone), and prolactin. The condition results from damage to or dysfunction of the anterior pituitary, most commonly caused by pituitary adenomas, their treatment (surgery or radiation), or infiltrative diseases. Clinically, hypopituitarism manifests as secondary adrenal insufficiency (low cortisol with inappropriately low ACTH), central hypothyroidism (low free T4 with low/inappropriately normal TSH), hypogonadotropic hypogonadism, and growth hormone deficiency. The management hierarchy is critical: glucocorticoids must always be replaced before thyroid hormone, because thyroid hormone accelerates cortisol metabolism and can precipitate adrenal crisis.

keyStats.prevalence: Estimated 300-500 per million people have hypopituitarism; the condition is underdiagnosed — many patients with pituitary tumors and their treatments develop partial or complete hormone deficiencies that are not fully evaluated
keyStats.demographics: Occurs across all ages; most commonly diagnosed in adults with pituitary adenomas (prolactinoma, non-functioning adenoma, GH-secreting tumors); Sheehan syndrome is the leading cause in women of reproductive age in low-resource settings
keyStats.avgAge: Peak diagnosis in the fourth and fifth decades of life; presentation age depends on cause — pituitary adenomas present in middle age, childhood craniopharyngiomas present much earlier
keyStats.globalCases: Pituitary adenomas (prevalence approximately 1 in 1000) are the most common pituitary disorder and the leading cause of hypopituitarism; hypothalamic-pituitary axis dysfunction from radiation affects 20-50% of brain-irradiated patients

definition: Hypopituitarism (ICD-10: E23.0) is a clinical syndrome resulting from inadequate secretion of anterior pituitary hormones — ACTH, TSH, FSH, LH, and GH — due to destruction or dysfunction of the anterior pituitary gland. The posterior pituitary (ADH and oxytocin) may be separately affected. The clinical picture reflects the pattern of hormone deficiencies: ACTH deficiency causes secondary adrenal insufficiency (low cortisol, low ACTH — distinguishing it from primary adrenal insufficiency where ACTH is high); TSH deficiency causes central (secondary) hypothyroidism with low free T4 and inappropriately low TSH; FSH/LH deficiency causes hypogonadotropic hypogonadism (low testosterone in men, amenorrhea in women); GH deficiency causes metabolic dysfunction and reduced bone density. The pituitary is the 'master gland' — its hormones regulate other endocrine glands (adrenal, thyroid, gonads) and metabolic processes (growth, metabolism). Pituitary failure therefore has cascading effects throughout the endocrine system. The condition can be partial (deficiency of one or two hormones) or complete (panhypopituitarism — all anterior pituitary hormones deficient). Deficiencies may be present at diagnosis or develop after treatment of the underlying cause.

typesClassification.description: Deficiency of one or two pituitary hormones with preserved function of the others. Common patterns include ACTH deficiency alone (most dangerous), TSH + ACTH deficiency, or gonadotropin deficiency with preserved GH. Partial deficiency may progress to complete panhypopituitarism over time.

Deficiency of all anterior pituitary hormones (ACTH, TSH, FSH/LH, GH, prolactin). Requires replacement of all deficient hormones for life. Can occur with large pituitary adenomas, pituitary infarction, or after radical surgery.

ACTH deficiency causing low cortisol with low ACTH — the hallmark of pituitary (not adrenal) adrenal insufficiency. Critical distinction: ACTH deficiency does not cause hyperpigmentation (ACTH is low), does not cause mineralocorticoid deficiency (aldosterone is renin-angiotensin controlled, not ACTH-dependent), and can be life-threatening if glucocorticoid replacement is not given before thyroid hormone.

TSH deficiency causing low free T4 with low or inappropriately normal TSH — distinguishing central hypothyroidism from primary hypothyroidism (where TSH is elevated). Central hypothyroidism does not cause hypercholesterolemia as prominently as primary hypothyroidism; treatment requires levothyroxine based on free T4 levels, not TSH.

FSH/LH deficiency causing low sex steroids (testosterone in men, estrogen in women) with low gonadotropins. Presents as erectile dysfunction and low testosterone in men, and amenorrhea/oligomenorrhea with low estrogen in women. Distinguishable from primary gonadal failure where gonadotropins are elevated.

GH deficiency in adults causes metabolic syndrome features: increased fat mass, reduced lean mass, reduced exercise capacity, dyslipidemia, and reduced bone mineral density. In children, GHD causes short stature. Adult GHD replacement improves quality of life and metabolic parameters.

primarySymptoms: Fatigue and exercise intolerance — cortisol deficiency and hypothyroidism both cause profound fatigue; patients report being unable to complete routine activities without rest.
Weight gain with reduced metabolic rate — central hypothyroidism reduces basal metabolic rate; cortisol deficiency also impairs stress response and energy metabolism.
Cold intolerance and slowed mental processing — hypothyroidism causes these symptoms directly; patients report needing extra blankets and experiencing mental fog.

Loss of libido and erectile dysfunction (men) or amenorrhea (women) — gonadotropin deficiency causes sex steroid deficiency; loss of morning erections and low testosterone in men; cessation of menses in premenopausal women.
Infertility and menstrual irregularities — hypogonadotropic hypogonadism prevents ovulation in women and impairs spermatogenesis in men; this is often the presenting symptom in younger patients.

Hypotension and orthostatic lightheadedness — ACTH deficiency causes secondary adrenal insufficiency; without cortisol's vasopressor effect and vascular tone, blood pressure falls, especially on standing.
Loss of body and axillary hair — cortisol and gonadal hormone deficiency reduces body hair; cortisol is required for normal hair follicle function.
Reduced sweating — cortisol deficiency reduces eccrine sweat gland function; combined with hypothyroidism, patients feel less able to thermoregulate.
Mood changes: depression, emotional blunting — cortisol deficiency and gonadal hormone deficiency both affect mood; patients may report loss of joy, flat affect, and social withdrawal.
earlyWarningSigns: Persistent fatigue despite adequate sleep — cortisol deficiency mimics 'adrenal fatigue' but is a serious medical condition requiring replacement; should prompt ACTH and cortisol testing
New-onset hypotension or orthostatic symptoms in a pituitary patient — suggests ACTH deficiency and possible adrenal crisis risk
Unexplained weight gain with cold intolerance — suggests central hypothyroidism; checking free T4 (not TSH) in a pituitary patient is the appropriate test
Loss of morning erection or decreased libido in a male with pituitary disease — suggests testosterone deficiency from gonadotropin deficiency; check morning testosterone and LH/FSH
Secondary amenorrhea (cessation of periods for 3+ months) in a woman not on contraceptives — warrants pituitary hormone evaluation including prolactin, TSH, and gonadotropins
emergencySigns: Adrenal crisis (acute secondary adrenal insufficiency) — presents with severe hypotension, nausea, vomiting, abdominal pain, confusion, hyponatremia, hypoglycemia — medical emergency requiring immediate IV hydrocortisone (100 mg bolus then continuous infusion or 50-100 mg every 6 hours); without treatment, it is fatal

Severe hyponatremia (serum sodium below 125 mEq/L) with confusion or seizures — cortisol deficiency impairs free water excretion and causes hypotonic hyponatremia; urgent glucocorticoid administration is required

Hypoglycemia (blood glucose below 50 mg/dL) with altered consciousness — cortisol and GH deficiency both impair gluconeogenesis and hypoglycemia counter-regulation; requires IV dextrose and glucocorticoids

Sheehan syndrome postpartum — massive postpartum hemorrhage causing pituitary infarction; presents with failure to lactate (prolactin deficiency), inability to resume menses (gonadotropin deficiency), fatigue and hypotension (ACTH deficiency) — requires urgent hormonal evaluation and replacement

Sudden severe headache with visual field defect — pituitary apoplexy (hemorrhage or infarction of pituitary adenoma) causing sudden hypopituitarism plus headache and potential vision loss — requires urgent MRI and neurosurgical evaluation

causes.description: Pituitary adenomas (prolactinomas, non-functioning adenomas, GH-secreting, ACTH-secreting tumors) are the most common cause of hypopituitarism. Macroadenomas (>1 cm) compress the normal pituitary and cause hormone deficiencies; treatment by transsphenoidal surgery or radiation can cause additional damage. Gaps in normal pituitary tissue after surgery, and radiation-induced hypothalamic-pituitary dysfunction, are major contributors.

Sudden hemorrhage or infarction of a pituitary adenoma, often precipitated by anticoagulation, hypertension, or dynamic tumor activity. Causes acute onset of severe headache, visual field defect, and sudden pituitary hormone deficiencies. May present as a medical emergency with adrenal crisis, hypotension, and hyponatremia. Requires urgent neurosurgical evaluation — some patients require emergent surgery, others respond to high-dose glucocorticoids and can be managed medically.

Transsphenoidal surgery for pituitary adenomas can damage the normal pituitary gland, particularly when adenomas are large, invasive, or removed completely. The rate of new hormone deficiency after transsphenoidal surgery varies from 5-20% depending on tumor size, surgical approach, and surgeon experience. Radiation therapy (conventional fractionated or stereotactic radiosurgery) causes progressive pituitary hormone loss — up to 50% of patients develop new deficiencies within 5 years of radiation.

Lymphocytic hypophysitis (autoimmune inflammation of the pituitary, often associated with pregnancy) causes pituitary enlargement and hormone deficiencies — most commonly ACTH and TSH deficiency. Sarcoidosis, histiocytosis, and granulomatosis with polyangiitis cause pituitary infiltration and dysfunction. Hemochromatosis (iron deposition) causes pituitary dysfunction with hypogonadotropic hypogonadism being the most common feature.

Massive postpartum hemorrhage causing hypovolemic shock and ischemic necrosis of the enlarged pituitary gland (which Physiologically enlarges during pregnancy). Presents after delivery with failure to lactate (prolactin deficiency), inability to resume menses (gonadotropin deficiency), fatigue and hypotension (ACTH deficiency), and hypothyroidism (TSH deficiency). Common in low-resource settings with limited obstetric care; an underdiagnosed cause of hypopituitarism in women of reproductive age worldwide.

TBI can cause hypopituitarism through direct damage to the hypothalamus or pituitary stalk, or through vascular injury to the portal system. Approximately 25-40% of severe TBI survivors develop some form of pituitary hormone deficiency, most commonly GH deficiency and gonadotropin deficiency. ACTH and TSH deficiency are less common but more clinically significant.

Cranial radiation (for brain tumors, head and neck cancers, or whole-brain irradiation for leukemia) causes progressive, dose-dependent damage to the hypothalamic-pituitary axis. The median effective dose for causing hypopituitarism is approximately 30-50 Gy. Deficiencies develop years after treatment (median 5-10 years) and accumulate over time — long-term follow-up is essential for all irradiated patients.

Congenital hypopituitarism (combined pituitary hormone deficiency, CPHD) from mutations in PROP1, POU1F1, LHX3, LHX4, and other transcription factors causes deficiency of multiple pituitary hormones from birth. Septo-optic dysplasia (mutation in HESX1) causes hypopituitarism plus optic nerve hypoplasia and midline brain defects. These typically present in childhood with short stature, hypoglycemia, and micropenis in boys.

riskFactors.description: Macroadenomas compress the normal pituitary gland and are the leading cause of hypopituitarism. The larger the tumor, the more likely multiple hormone deficiencies are present at diagnosis.

Transsphenoidal surgery for pituitary tumors can cause new hormone deficiencies in 5-20% of patients. The risk is higher with larger tumors, invasive tumors, and less experienced surgeons. Pre-operative and post-operative pituitary function testing is essential.

Radiation-induced hypopituitarism develops in a dose-dependent manner over years. Patients who have received cranial radiation (for brain tumors, head and neck cancers) should have annual pituitary function testing for life.

Moderate-to-severe TBI causes hypopituitarism in 25-40% of survivors. The risk is highest in patients with prolonged ICU admission, cranial fractures, or diffuse axonal injury. All TBI survivors should be screened for pituitary deficiencies at 3-6 months post-injury.

Massive postpartum hemorrhage causing hypovolemic shock is the key risk factor for Sheehan syndrome. All women with severe postpartum hemorrhage should be monitored for pituitary dysfunction in the postpartum period.

riskFactors.factor: Systemic inflammatory diseases (sarcoidosis, Langerhans cell histiocytosis)
riskFactors.description: Sarcoidosis and other granulomatous diseases can infiltrate the pituitary and hypothalamus, causing hypopituitarism. Serum ACE and chest X-ray are appropriate screening tests for sarcoidosis in patients with unexplained pituitary dysfunction.

Iron deposition in the pituitary causes hypogonadotropic hypogonadism (the most common manifestation), and can also cause ACTH and TSH deficiency in advanced disease. Serum ferritin and transferrin saturation screening in patients with unexplained hypopituitarism is appropriate.

affectedDemographics: Hypopituitarism affects all ages and both sexes; pituitary adenomas (the leading cause) are most common in adults aged 30-50, with no significant sex difference
Sheehan syndrome occurs exclusively in women of reproductive age who have experienced severe postpartum hemorrhage
Childhood craniopharyngiomas and congenital hypopituitarism cause hypopituitarism presenting in the first two decades of life
Radiation-induced hypopituitarism affects survivors of childhood and adult brain tumors and head/neck cancers who received cranial radiation
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis of hypopituitarism requires hormonal testing of each pituitary axis, followed by identification of the underlying cause with imaging (MRI of the pituitary). Testing strategy: (1) Assess ACTH deficiency: morning cortisol (8 AM) — a level below 100-150 nmol/L (3-5 mcg/dL) suggests deficiency and warrants ACTH stimulation testing; ACTH is low in secondary adrenal insufficiency. (2) Assess TSH/thyroid axis: free T4 (low) with TSH that is low, normal, or mildly elevated (inappropriately normal for a low free T4) confirms central hypothyroidism; primary hypothyroidism would have elevated TSH. (3) Assess gonadotropin axis: in men, morning testosterone with LH/FSH; in women, LH/FSH with estradiol. Low testosterone with inappropriately low LH/FSH indicates hypogonadotropic hypogonadism. (4) Assess GH axis: IGF-1 is the screening test for GH deficiency — low IGF-1 suggests GHD and warrants stimulation testing (insulin tolerance test or GHRH-arginine test) for confirmation. (5) Assess prolactin: elevated prolactin suggests prolactinoma; low prolactin may indicate pituitary damage. MRI of the pituitary and hypothalamus with contrast identifies structural causes (adenoma, infiltration, infarction). Dynamic testing (ACTH stimulation, GH stimulation) is required for borderline cases.

diagnosticTests.purpose: The first-line test for ACTH/cortisol deficiency. Cortisol follows a diurnal rhythm, peaking at 8 AM. A morning cortisol above 500 nmol/L (18 mcg/dL) effectively excludes adrenal insufficiency; a level below 100 nmol/L (3.5 mcg/dL) strongly suggests deficiency. Values between 100-500 nmol/L require ACTH stimulation testing.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard blood draw at 8 AM (patient should be fasting, rested, and not have taken glucocorticoids). Results within hours if sent to hospital lab. An early morning sample is essential — cortisol drawn later in the day may be falsely reassuring.

diagnosticTests.purpose: The gold standard for confirming adrenal insufficiency. Measure serum cortisol at baseline and 30 and 60 minutes after IV/IM synacthen (250 mcg). Peak cortisol below 500 nmol/L (18 mcg/dL) confirms adrenal insufficiency; ACTH level at baseline distinguishes primary (high ACTH) from secondary (low ACTH).
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: IV cannula placed; baseline cortisol drawn; 250 mcg synacthen injected IV or IM; further blood draws at 30 and 60 minutes. Takes approximately 90 minutes total. Safe and well-tolerated. In hypopituitarism, cortisol fails to rise appropriately (secondary insufficiency).

diagnosticTests.purpose: Free T4 (not TSH alone) is the appropriate test for central hypothyroidism. In ACTH deficiency, TSH may be 'normal' even though free T4 is low — this is because TSH secretion is appropriate for the low T4 in primary hypothyroidism but is inappropriately low for the low T4 in central hypothyroidism.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard blood draw, no fasting required. Results same day or next day. Low free T4 with low/inappropriately normal TSH confirms central hypothyroidism. TSH alone is misleading in pituitary patients — always order free T4 simultaneously.

diagnosticTests.test: Testosterone, LH, and FSH (men); Estradiol, LH, FSH (women)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Evaluate gonadotropin axis. Low testosterone in men with low or normal LH/FSH indicates hypogonadotropic hypogonadism. In women, low estradiol with low LH/FSH indicates the same. These patterns distinguish pituitary (secondary) hypogonadism from primary gonadal failure where gonadotropins are elevated.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Morning blood draw (8-10 AM) for testosterone in men — testosterone has a diurnal rhythm with peak in early morning. Results in 1-2 days. In men with hypopituitarism, morning testosterone is typically below the reference range with LH/FSH in the normal or low range.

diagnosticTests.purpose: The screening test for GH deficiency in adults. IGF-1 reflects integrated GH secretion and is more stable than GH itself (which is pulsatile and suppressed by glucose). Low IGF-1 for age suggests GHD and warrants dynamic stimulation testing to confirm.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard blood draw, no special preparation required. IGF-1 is stable throughout the day. Results in 1-2 weeks. Age-adjusted reference ranges are essential — IGF-1 that is 'normal' in a 20-year-old may be low for a 60-year-old. A low IGF-1 in a pituitary patient is highly suggestive of GHD.

diagnosticTests.test: GH stimulation test (insulin tolerance test or GHRH-arginine)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Confirmatory test for GH deficiency. ITT is the gold standard: insulin-induced hypoglycemia stimulates GH secretion. Peak GH below 3 ng/mL (or 5 ng/mL in some guidelines) confirms GHD. GHRH-arginine is an alternative for patients in whom hypoglycemia is contraindicated.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Requires IV insulin to induce hypoglycemia (blood glucose below 2.2 mmol/L or 40 mg/dL); GH measured at baseline and 30, 60, and 90 minutes. Must be performed in a monitored setting with IV dextrose available. Contraindicated in patients with seizure disorders, coronary artery disease, or severe hypopituitarism without glucocorticoid replacement.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Essential for identifying the structural cause of hypopituitarism. MRI with gadolinium contrast demonstrates pituitary adenomas, cystic lesions (Rathke's cleft cysts, craniopharyngiomas), infiltration (lymphocytic hypophysitis, sarcoidosis), and infarction (Sheehan syndrome showing empty sella).
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: MRI of the sella turcica with thin cuts and gadolinium contrast, taking 30-45 minutes. Patients with metal implants or severe claustrophobia may not be suitable. Non-invasive. MRI identifies the cause in the majority of cases and is essential for surgical planning.

treatmentOverview: Treatment of hypopituitarism requires replacement of all deficient hormones in a physiologically appropriate manner. The critical hierarchy is: glucocorticoids FIRST, then thyroid hormone, then sex steroids, then GH. Glucocorticoid deficiency is immediately life-threatening — untreated adrenal crisis is fatal. Thyroid hormone replacement must not be started until glucocorticoids are established, because thyroid hormone accelerates cortisol metabolism and can precipitate adrenal crisis. Glucocorticoid replacement: hydrocortisone 10-20 mg in the morning and 5-10 mg in the early afternoon replicates the diurnal cortisol rhythm. Some patients need a third dose. Mineralocorticoid (fludrocortisone) is NOT needed in secondary adrenal insufficiency because aldosterone is regulated by the renin-angiotensin system, not ACTH. Thyroid replacement: levothyroxine started at 1.6 mcg/kg/day (lower in elderly) with monitoring of free T4 to keep in the upper half of the reference range. Sex steroid replacement: testosterone gel/patch/injection for men; transdermal estradiol with cyclic progesterone for women of reproductive age. GH replacement: daily subcutaneous GH (0.2-0.5 mg/day) for adults with confirmed GHD and no contraindications. All patients need a medical alert ID and emergency glucocorticoid protocol.

medicalTreatments.name: Hydrocortisone 10-20 mg AM / 5-10 mg early PM
medicalTreatments.description: Glucocorticoid replacement for secondary adrenal insufficiency. Hydrocortisone (cortisol) is the physiological replacement. Divided dosing (morning + early afternoon) mimics the natural diurnal rhythm. Typical total daily dose is 15-25 mg. Higher doses are not needed because aldosterone is preserved (renin-angiotensin system is intact).
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Controls fatigue, hypotension, and orthostatic symptoms; prevents adrenal crisis. Does not restore normal cortisol circadian rhythm fully (physiological cortisol has additional evening nadir). Patients should be educated about stress-dosing: double or triple hydrocortisone during illness, fever above 38°C, or injury.

medicalTreatments.name: Levothyroxine (T4) 1.6 mcg/kg/day
medicalTreatments.description: Thyroid hormone replacement for central hypothyroidism. Levothyroxine (synthetic T4) is converted to T3 in peripheral tissues. Starting dose is 1.6 mcg/kg/day in adults; lower starting doses (0.5-1 mcg/kg) for elderly or cardiac patients. Monitor free T4 to guide dose titration.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Corrects hypothyroidism — improved energy, temperature tolerance, and mental function. IMPORTANT: Must not be started until glucocorticoid replacement is established — thyroid hormone increases cortisol metabolism and can precipitate adrenal crisis in untreated patients.

medicalTreatments.name: Testosterone replacement (men)
medicalTreatments.description: Testosterone gel, patch, or IM injection for hypogonadal men. Options: testosterone gel (1% or 1.62%, applied daily to skin), testosterone patch (applied nightly to back or thigh), or testosterone enanthate/cypionate IM every 2-4 weeks. Target testosterone level in the mid-to-upper physiological range.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Restores libido, erectile function, energy, and mood; increases lean body mass; improves bone mineral density. Takes 3-6 months for full effect. Monitor hematocrit (testosterone increases red cell mass) and PSA (in men over 45).

medicalTreatments.name: Estradiol replacement (women)
medicalTreatments.description: Transdermal estradiol patches or gel for women with hypogonadotropic hypogonadism. Add cyclic micronized progesterone (100-200 mg at night for 12 days per month) in women with an intact uterus to protect the endometrium. Oral estradiol is acceptable but transdermal is preferred (avoids first-pass hepatic metabolism).
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Restores estrogen levels, preventing menopausal symptoms (hot flashes, vaginal dryness), bone loss, and cardiovascular risk. Cyclic progesterone withdrawal produces regular menses and protects the endometrium. Transdermal route is safer (lower clotting risk) than oral.

medicalTreatments.name: Growth hormone replacement (somatropin)
medicalTreatments.description: Daily subcutaneous injections of recombinant human GH for adults with confirmed GHD and no contraindications. Dose: 0.2-0.5 mg/day (0.3-1.0 IU/day), titrated based on IGF-1 levels and clinical response. GH replacement in adults is approved for severe GHD (peak GH <3 ng/mL on stimulation testing).
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Improves quality of life (energy, mood), increases lean body mass, reduces fat mass, improves exercise capacity, increases bone mineral density. IGF-1 is monitored to keep in the age-appropriate mid-range. Benefits take 6-12 months to fully manifest.

surgicalOptions.name: Transsphenoidal surgery for pituitary adenoma
surgicalOptions.description: Endoscopic or microscopic transsphenoidal resection of pituitary adenoma. Indicated for macroadenomas causing mass effect (visual field compression), non-functioning adenomas causing hypopituitarism, or GH-secreting adenomas not controlled medically. Can improve or stabilize hormone deficiencies depending on surgical success and pre-existing damage.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Endoscopic transsphenoidal surgery has high rates of tumor control (70-90%) and low complication rates (CSF leak 3-5%, permanent DI 2-3%, new hormone deficiency 5-10%). Surgery can improve visual field defects in 80-90% of patients.

surgicalOptions.name: Decompressive surgery for pituitary apoplexy
surgicalOptions.description: Urgent surgical decompression for pituitary apoplexy with visual field defect, declining consciousness, or failure to respond to high-dose glucocorticoids. Usually combined with transsphenoidal approach. Early surgery (within 1 week) improves visual and endocrine outcomes.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Surgical decompression improves visual field defects in 70-80% of patients with apoplexy. Endocrine outcome is less predictable — approximately 50% of patients have some improvement in pituitary function, 30% remain stable, and 20% worsen.

alternativeTreatments.name: DHEA supplementation (adrenal androgen replacement)
alternativeTreatments.description: DHEA (dehydroepiandrosterone), the precursor to androgens, is secreted by the adrenal cortex and declines in hypopituitarism. Some studies suggest DHEA replacement (25-50 mg/day) improves mood, energy, and sexual function in adrenal insufficient patients.
alternativeTreatments.effectiveness: Modest benefit in some patients for energy and sexual function; not a standard treatment but considered in patients with persistent symptoms despite adequate glucocorticoid, thyroid, and sex steroid replacement.

whySeeSpecialist: An endocrinologist is the essential specialist for managing hypopituitarism — they coordinate the complex replacement regimen, monitor hormone levels, and manage complications. A neurosurgeon is required for pituitary tumors requiring surgical management. An ophthalmologist evaluates visual field defects. A radiation oncologist manages radiation treatment for residual tumor. An emergency medicine physician must be educated about the need for stress-dose glucocorticoids in adrenal-insufficient patients presenting with acute illness. A dietitian can advise on nutritional management in patients with metabolic complications.
doctorSelectionGuide: Look for an endocrinologist with specific expertise in pituitary disorders — they should be comfortable managing complex hormone replacement regimens and have access to pituitary MRI and dynamic hormone testing. Academic pituitary centers typically have the most experience with the full spectrum of pituitary disease including surgery, radiation, and complex replacement. Ask about familiarity with GH replacement in adults and the specific requirements for stress-dose glucocorticoid education.
preventionStrategies: Pre-operative pituitary hormone testing in all patients with pituitary adenomas — establish baseline endocrine function before surgery or radiation to guide post-treatment monitoring
Annual pituitary hormone testing after pituitary surgery or cranial radiation — hormone deficiencies develop over time and annual surveillance is essential
Genetic counseling for patients with congenital hypopituitarism — PROP1, POU1F1, and other CPHD mutations have autosomal recessive inheritance patterns and implications for siblings
Medical alert identification and education — all patients with ACTH deficiency must carry emergency information and be educated about stress-dose glucocorticoid protocols
lifestyleModifications: Wear a medical alert bracelet or necklace identifying adrenal insufficiency and the need for IV hydrocortisone in emergencies

Carry a hydrocortisone emergency injection kit (100 mg hydrocortisone for IM use) — available in pre-filled syringes for patients with known adrenal insufficiency who may not be able to take oral medication during illness
Inform all healthcare providers (including dentists) of pituitary hormone deficiencies before any procedure — stress-dose glucocorticoids are required for surgery and invasive procedures
Stay up to date with vaccinations (influenza, pneumococcal) — infection is a common precipitant of adrenal crisis in hypopituitary patients
Exercise regularly — once on appropriate hormone replacement, regular aerobic exercise is safe and beneficial; improves metabolic health and cardiovascular risk

recommended: Balanced diet with adequate protein (0.8-1.0 g/kg/day) to support muscle mass — particularly important for patients with GH deficiency who have reduced lean body mass

Calcium and vitamin D supplementation — patients on glucocorticoid replacement (even physiological doses) and those with gonadal hormone deficiency benefit from calcium (1000-1200 mg/day) and vitamin D (1000-2000 IU/day) for bone health

Regular meals to prevent hypoglycemia — patients with combined ACTH and GH deficiency have impaired gluconeogenesis and are more prone to hypoglycemia between meals; avoiding prolonged fasting is important
avoid: Excessive sodium intake — patients with secondary adrenal insufficiency do not need mineralocorticoid replacement (aldosterone is preserved), but excessive sodium intake is not recommended for cardiovascular health
Skipping meals — the combination of cortisol and GH deficiency impairs glucose counter-regulation; skipping meals can cause hypoglycemia
Excessive alcohol — alcohol increases hypoglycemia risk and can worsen osteoporosis in patients with gonadal hormone deficiency
Raw iodine supplementation in excess — not relevant for hypopituitarism; thyroid replacement is with levothyroxine, not iodine

exerciseGuidelines: Exercise is safe and strongly recommended for hypopituitary patients on appropriate replacement therapy — regular aerobic exercise (150 minutes/week) improves cardiovascular risk, mood, and metabolic parameters. Patients with untreated adrenal insufficiency or uncorrected hypothyroidism should avoid strenuous exercise until hormone replacement is optimized. GH-deficient patients may have reduced exercise capacity that improves with GH replacement. Patients with osteoporosis from gonadal hormone deficiency should include weight-bearing exercise to support bone density. During adrenal insufficiency (illness days), reduce exercise to mild activity only.
dailyManagement: Take hydrocortisone in divided doses (morning + early afternoon) — consistency in timing matters for mimicking physiological rhythm
Take levothyroxine on an empty stomach, 30-60 minutes before breakfast — calcium, iron, and fiber supplements interfere with absorption; take levothyroxine separately
Apply testosterone gel or patches as directed — testosterone gel is applied daily to upper arms or torso; patches are changed every 24 hours

Monitor for signs of over- or under-replacement: weight gain, moon face, striae (over-replacement) or persistent fatigue, hypotension, nausea (under-replacement) — share concerns with your endocrinologist
Get a medical alert bracelet identifying adrenal insufficiency — this is essential for emergency medical personnel
Have an emergency hydrocortisone injection kit at home and when traveling — know how and when to use it; instructions are provided with the kit
prognosis: The prognosis of hypopituitarism depends primarily on the underlying cause and the adequacy of hormone replacement. With appropriate replacement of all deficient hormones, life expectancy approaches normal and quality of life is substantially improved. The key determinant of outcomes is whether glucocorticoid replacement is adequate — untreated adrenal insufficiency causes adrenal crisis and death; properly managed patients have no excess mortality from the cortisol deficiency axis. The most common cause of excess mortality in hypopituitary patients is cardiovascular disease — this may be related to untreated GH deficiency, inadequate glucocorticoid replacement, or the underlying condition itself. Long-term studies show that patients with hypopituitarism on adequate replacement therapy have near-normal life expectancy but elevated cardiovascular risk requiring aggressive management of metabolic risk factors. Patients with childhood-onset hypopituitarism from craniopharyngioma or congenital causes have additional considerations around neurocognitive development and social integration.
recoveryTimeline: Hormone replacement begins immediately upon diagnosis. Cortisol (hydrocortisone) takes effect within hours — fatigue, hypotension, and nausea improve within days. Thyroid hormone (levothyroxine) takes 4-6 weeks for full effect; symptoms gradually improve over this period. Testosterone replacement takes 3-6 months for full effect on sexual function, mood, and body composition. GH replacement takes 6-12 months for maximum metabolic and quality-of-life benefits. During the initial treatment phase, close monitoring of symptoms and hormone levels (after appropriate time intervals for each hormone) is essential to titrate doses correctly.
complications: Adrenal crisis (acute secondary adrenal insufficiency) — the most life-threatening complication; triggered by infection, surgery, trauma, or missed glucocorticoid doses; presents with hypotension, nausea, vomiting, confusion, hyponatremia, hypoglycemia; requires immediate IV hydrocortisone 100 mg bolus; preventable with proper patient education and emergency protocols

Cardiovascular disease — hypopituitary patients have elevated cardiovascular mortality compared to the general population; contributors include untreated GH deficiency (dyslipidemia, increased fat mass), glucocorticoid over-replacement, and the underlying condition; aggressive management of blood pressure, lipids, and metabolic risk factors is essential

Osteoporosis and fractures — sex steroid deficiency and GH deficiency both reduce bone mineral density; patients with hypogonadism and/or GHD have elevated fracture risk; adequate sex steroid and GH replacement (when indicated) reduces this risk

Metabolic syndrome — GH deficiency and sex steroid deficiency contribute to increased fat mass, reduced muscle mass, and insulin resistance; GH replacement improves metabolic parameters in many patients

Neurocognitive impairment — childhood hypopituitarism (from craniopharyngioma or congenital causes) is associated with cognitive deficits, particularly working memory and executive function; early hormone replacement optimizes neurodevelopmental outcomes

Infertility — hypogonadotropic hypogonadism causes infertility in both sexes; gonadotropin therapy (FSH/LH injections) can restore fertility in many patients and is managed by reproductive endocrinology specialists

supportResources.name: Pituitary Society — Patient Resources
supportResources.description: Resources for pituitary patients including information on hypopituitarism, adrenal crisis prevention, and finding pituitary specialists. Includes guidelines for patients on glucocorticoid replacement.

supportResources.name: The Endocrine Society — Patient Education
supportResources.description: Evidence-based patient information on pituitary disorders and hormonal replacement from the leading professional organization in endocrinology.

supportResources.name: National Adrenal Diseases Foundation (NADF)
supportResources.description: Support and education for patients with adrenal insufficiency, including secondary adrenal insufficiency from hypopituitarism. Includes emergency protocol information.

confusedWithConditions.name: Primary Adrenal Insufficiency (Addison disease)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Both cause low cortisol but Addison disease has HIGH ACTH (the pituitary makes more ACTH trying to stimulate a failing adrenal gland) while hypopituitaris has LOW ACTH. Addison disease causes hyperpigmentation (high ACTH stimulates melanocytes), salt craving, and hyperkalemia (aldosterone is deficient). Secondary adrenal insufficiency from hypopituitarism has preserved aldosterone (no hyperkalemia or salt craving) and no hyperpigmentation.

confusedWithConditions.name: Primary Hypothyroidism
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Primary hypothyroidism has HIGH TSH (the pituitary makes more TSH trying to stimulate a failing thyroid gland); central hypothyroidism from hypopituitarism has LOW or inappropriately normal TSH (the pituitary itself is damaged). Both cause low free T4. Distinguishing them matters because treatment of central hypothyroidism requires free T4 monitoring (not TSH), and thyroid hormone must not be started before glucocorticoid replacement in central hypothyroidism.

confusedWithConditions.name: Polycystic Ovary Syndrome (PCOS)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: PCOS causes oligomenorrhea and hyperandrogenism (acne, hirsutism) with elevated testosterone and LH (typically normal-to-high); hypogonadotropic hypogonadism causes low estradiol and low LH/FSH. In PCOS, LH is often elevated relative to FSH; in hypopituitarism, both are low. The pubertal development pattern differs: PCOS patients had normal puberty; hypopituitarism patients may have had delayed or absent puberty.

confusedWithConditions.name: Chronic Fatigue Syndrome (CFS)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: CFS causes fatigue without the endocrine abnormalities of hypopituitarism — cortisol is normal or mildly low in CFS (not as severely low as in adrenal insufficiency), TSH and free T4 are normal, and sex hormones are normal. Hypopituitarism causes specific hormonal patterns identifiable on testing. CFS does not cause hypotension, cold intolerance, or the specific hormone deficiencies of hypopituitarism.

relatedConditions.name: Pituitary Adenoma
relatedConditions.relevance: Pituitary adenomas (prolactinomas, non-functioning adenomas, GH-secreting, ACTH-secreting tumors) are the most common cause of hypopituitarism. The adenoma itself may produce excess hormone (prolactin, GH, ACTH) while compressing the normal pituitary, causing deficiency of other hormones. Treatment of the adenoma may improve or worsen hypopituitarism.

relatedConditions.name: Sheehan Syndrome
relatedConditions.relevance: Postpartum pituitary necrosis from massive hemorrhage causing hypopituitarism. Presents with failure to lactate (prolactin deficiency), amenorrhea (gonadotropin deficiency), fatigue and hypotension (ACTH deficiency), and hypothyroidism (TSH deficiency). A distinct cause of hypopituitarism unique to women of reproductive age.

relatedConditions.name: Craniopharyngioma
relatedConditions.relevance: Benign cystic tumor of the Rathke's pouch region causing hypopituitarism (often multiple hormone deficiencies), visual field defects, and headaches. Common in children (peak 5-15 years) and adults (peak 50-70 years). Treated by surgical resection and radiation; hypopituitarism is common after treatment.

relatedConditions.name: Lymphocytic Hypophysitis
relatedConditions.relevance: Autoimmune inflammation of the pituitary causing pituitary enlargement and hormone deficiencies — most commonly ACTH and TSH deficiency. Often associated with pregnancy (lymphocytic hypophysitis of pregnancy). MRI shows pituitary enlargement mimicking a tumor; biopsy is diagnostic but rarely needed — clinical presentation and follow-up imaging guide management.

faqs.question: What is hypopituitarism?
faqs.answer: Hypopituitarism is deficiency of one or more pituitary hormones — including cortisol (ACTH), thyroid hormone (TSH), sex hormones (FSH/LH), and growth hormone. It results from damage to the pituitary gland, most commonly from pituitary tumors, surgery, or radiation. Each hormone deficiency has specific consequences: cortisol deficiency is immediately life-threatening; TSH deficiency causes hypothyroidism; FSH/LH deficiency causes infertility and low sex hormones.

faqs.question: How is hypopituitarism treated?
faqs.answer: Each deficient hormone is replaced individually. The critical rule is: glucocorticoids FIRST, then thyroid hormone, then sex steroids, then growth hormone. Hydrocortisone replaces cortisol; levothyroxine replaces thyroid hormone; testosterone or estrogen replaces sex hormones; GH injections replace growth hormone. All replacements are lifelong.

faqs.question: What is the difference between primary and secondary adrenal insufficiency?
faqs.answer: Primary adrenal insufficiency (Addison disease) is failure of the adrenal gland itself — ACTH is HIGH (trying to stimulate the failing adrenal). Secondary adrenal insufficiency is ACTH deficiency from pituitary damage — ACTH is LOW. The distinction matters: secondary insufficiency does not affect aldosterone (so no salt craving or hyperkalemia), and ACTH is low (so no hyperpigmentation). Both require glucocorticoid replacement.

faqs.question: What is central hypothyroidism?
faqs.answer: Central (secondary) hypothyroidism is low thyroid hormone caused by TSH deficiency (not thyroid gland failure). In primary hypothyroidism, TSH is HIGH. In central hypothyroidism, TSH is low or 'inappropriately normal' for the low free T4. Free T4 is the appropriate monitoring test for central hypothyroidism, not TSH.

faqs.question: Is hypopituitarism life-threatening?
faqs.answer: Yes — the ACTH/cortisol deficiency axis is immediately life-threatening. Without glucocorticoid replacement, patients are at risk for adrenal crisis during stress (infection, surgery, injury). Adrenal crisis causes severe hypotension, confusion, and death if untreated. With proper glucocorticoid replacement and emergency education, life expectancy is normal.

faqs.question: What is an adrenal crisis?
faqs.answer: An adrenal crisis is acute severe cortisol deficiency, triggered by infection, surgery, trauma, or missed glucocorticoid doses. Symptoms include severe weakness, nausea, vomiting, abdominal pain, hypotension, confusion, and hypoglycemia. It is a medical emergency treated with immediate IV hydrocortisone (100 mg bolus, then continuous infusion or 50-100 mg every 6 hours). Patients with hypopituitarism should carry emergency information and an emergency injection kit.

faqs.question: Can hypopituitarism be cured?
faqs.answer: Hypopituitarism from structural causes (adenoma, surgery, radiation, infiltration) is typically permanent and requires lifelong hormone replacement. Hypopituitarism from transient causes (lymphocytic hypophysitis, certain medications, mild traumatic brain injury) may be partially or fully reversible. In most patients, hormone replacement is the long-term management strategy.

faqs.question: Why does thyroid hormone need to wait for glucocorticoid replacement?
faqs.answer: Thyroid hormone increases the metabolism of cortisol (increases hepatic glucocorticoid clearance). If thyroid hormone is started before glucocorticoid replacement, the body is suddenly metabolizing whatever low cortisol it has more rapidly, potentially precipitating adrenal crisis. Always establish glucocorticoid replacement before starting thyroid hormone.

faqs.question: Do patients with hypopituitarism need mineralocorticoid replacement?
faqs.answer: No — mineralocorticoid (fludrocortisone) is only needed in primary adrenal insufficiency. In secondary adrenal insufficiency (ACTH deficiency), the adrenal gland is intact and aldosterone production is normal (regulated by the renin-angiotensin system, not ACTH). Replacing mineralocorticoid unnecessarily can cause hypertension and hypokalemia.

faqs.question: What causes hypopituitarism?
faqs.answer: The most common cause is a pituitary adenoma (or its treatment). Other causes include pituitary apoplexy (bleeding into an adenoma), infiltrative diseases (lymphocytic hypophysitis, sarcoidosis), traumatic brain injury, cranial radiation, Sheehan syndrome (postpartum pituitary necrosis), and congenital genetic disorders affecting pituitary development.

faqs.question: Can patients with hypopituitarism have children?
faqs.answer: Yes — hypogonadotropic hypogonadism causing infertility can be treated with gonadotropin therapy (FSH/LH injections) to stimulate ovulation in women and spermatogenesis in men. This is managed by reproductive endocrinology specialists. Many hypopituitary patients achieve pregnancy with appropriate treatment.

faqs.question: What is Sheehan syndrome?
faqs.answer: Sheehan syndrome is hypopituitarism caused by postpartum hemorrhage — massive blood loss causes hypovolemic shock and ischemic necrosis of the enlarged pituitary gland (which physiologically enlarges during pregnancy). It presents after delivery with failure to lactate (prolactin deficiency), inability to resume menses (gonadotropin deficiency), fatigue (ACTH deficiency), and cold intolerance (TSH deficiency).

faqs.question: Does growth hormone replacement work in adults with GHD?
faqs.answer: Yes — GH replacement in adults with confirmed GHD improves energy, mood, and quality of life; increases lean body mass; reduces fat mass; improves bone mineral density; and improves exercise capacity. IGF-1 is monitored to keep levels in the age-appropriate mid-range. Benefits take 6-12 months to fully manifest.

faqs.question: How do you test for pituitary hormone deficiencies?
faqs.answer: Each axis is tested separately: morning cortisol for ACTH deficiency (with ACTH stimulation test if borderline); free T4 and TSH for central hypothyroidism; testosterone/LH/FSH for gonadotropin deficiency; IGF-1 for GH deficiency (with GH stimulation test if low). Pituitary MRI identifies structural causes. Dynamic testing (ACTH stimulation, GH stimulation) confirms borderline cases.

faqs.question: What is panhypopituitarism?
faqs.answer: Panhypopituitarism is complete failure of the anterior pituitary — all hormones (ACTH, TSH, FSH, LH, GH) are deficient. Requires replacement of all hormones in the correct hierarchy (glucocorticoids first, then thyroid, then sex steroids, then GH). Patients are at risk for adrenal crisis and require emergency education.

faqs.question: Does hypopituitarism affect life expectancy?
faqs.answer: With appropriate hormone replacement and emergency protocols, life expectancy is near-normal. However, hypopituitary patients have elevated cardiovascular mortality — this may relate to untreated GH deficiency, metabolic risk factors, and the underlying condition. Aggressive management of cardiovascular risk factors is essential.

faqs.question: What is a medical alert ID for hypopituitarism?
faqs.answer: A medical alert bracelet or necklace that identifies the wearer as having adrenal insufficiency and requiring IV hydrocortisone in an emergency. This is essential because emergency personnel who see a collapsed patient need to know immediately that giving IV hydrocortisone is the priority. Patients should also carry an emergency hydrocortisone injection kit.

faqs.question: Why do patients with hypopituitarism need stress doses of glucocorticoids?
faqs.answer: The adrenal glands need ACTH to produce cortisol, and the pituitary provides ACTH. In hypopituitarism, ACTH is low, so cortisol production depends on glucocorticoid medication — which provides a fixed dose. During physical stress (fever, infection, surgery, injury), the body normally produces much more cortisol; without stress-dose glucocorticoids, patients can develop adrenal crisis. The rule is: double or triple the usual dose for fever above 38C, illness, or injury; IV hydrocortisone is needed for surgery or severe illness.

metaDescription: Hypopituitarism: deficiency of pituitary hormones (ACTH, TSH, FSH/LH, GH) causing adrenal insufficiency, hypothyroidism, infertility. Glucocorticoid replacement first, then thyroid hormone. Treatment hierarchy explained.
